main_sections.title: The Unlikely Best Friends
main_sections.prose_summary: Gajaraj is a royal elephant who lives in comfortable stables but feels deeply lonely, longing for a friend to play with. One evening a stray dog wanders into the stable. Gajaraj shares his food and the dog falls asleep there. The mahout, noticing the elephant's happiness, tolerates the dog's presence and even throws it crumbs. The two animals quickly become inseparable — they bathe together at the pond, the elephant playfully spraying the dog with his trunk, and on the way home Gajaraj lifts the dog onto his broad back for a ride. Their friendship is disrupted when a passing farmer recognises the dog as his own lost pet, Buntee, and leads him away on a rope. Neither animal can conceal its grief: both Gajaraj and Buntee refuse all food after the separation. The mahout suspects Gajaraj is pining for his friend; the farmer, seeing the same in Buntee, generously removes the rope and lets the dog go. Buntee races back to the stable. Gajaraj swings him joyfully with his trunk and both eat at last. The story closes with the mahout and the farmer embracing — a second unlikely friendship formed between two humans watching over their animals. The tale is adapted from the Jataka story of 'The Elephant and the Dog' and is attributed to Subba Rao (kathakids.com). The central theme is that genuine friendship crosses all boundaries of species and circumstance and is defined by mutual care, selfless giving, and the pain of separation.

listening.transcript_text: Let me tell you a story of a cow and a leopard from Vadodara in Gujarat who became friends. As you know, a leopard is a wild animal and a cow is a domestic animal. Some people say that the cow took care of the leopard cub like a mother. When the cub became older, it went away from the village. It returned to meet the cow at night. The villagers were worried but they never stopped the leopard. They saw that the leopard just sat beside the cow quietly. The cow and leopard spent time by relaxing and playing with each other. The leopard did not like the villagers and stopped coming regularly. The villagers were surprised that the leopard never attacked the cow but became its friend.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a story about 'Unlikely Friends'. Use exact words from the story to fill up the missing details in the paragraph.

question_bank.stem: The leopard is a ___________ animal but it became a friend of a cow. When the leopard grew up, it left the village. It came in the _______________ to meet the cow. The leopard would sit __________________ the cow. They also played together. _____________ were surprised at their friendship.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, listen to the story again and number the sentences in correct order. The first one has been done for you.

question_bank.stem: Number the sentences in correct order: (1) The cow took care of the leopard cub as a mother. (?) The leopard stopped coming regularly. (?) The leopard came to meet the cow at night. (?) The leopard left the village after growing up.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Gajaraj and Buntee had a wonderful time with each other even if they were 'unlikely' friends. Talk to your friend and find out your common likes and dislikes. Share your answers with the class. Fill in: I am ____________ / I like: ____________ / My friend is ____________ / My friend likes: ____________ / Both of us like: ____________. Now, tell your classmates about your friend using the cues given: My friend and I like to play. Both of us want ___________. / I like to ___________ but my friend likes to ___________. / We are different as she/he is ___________ and I am ___________.

Write six sentences about your friend with the help of the facts collected in the above task. Mention how you are not only similar but also different. You may take help of the words given below: same, different, and, but, both, still, like, unlike, neither, nor, either, or.

Gajaraj was sad without a friend and when he met Buntee, he was filled with joy. 'Sad' and 'filled with joy' are opposites. Opposites can also be formed by adding some letters before a word. For example, un- unhappy, in- incorrect, dis- disconnect, mis- misuse. Now, take help from the words given as hints and fill up their opposites in the puzzle. (Note: All the words are from the story you have just read.) Across: 3. unkind, 7. yelped, 8. awake, 9. relieved, 10. missing. Down: 1. started, 2. appeared, 4. sad, 5. untouched, 6. hate.

question_bank.stem: Complete the crossword puzzle with opposites of the given clue words.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, complete the paragraph with some of the words you have used in the puzzle. My pencil was _____________. I was very ________________ as it was a gift from my friend. I woke up my elder brother who was ____________ on the bed. I told him that my pencil seemed to have ________________. He _____________ and told me to look under my pillow. I was _________________ when I ______________ the pencil there.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The words that tell us about an action are called verbs. In the story, the author tells us what happened before. To do that, the past form of the action word (verb) is used. Let us learn about the three forms of past tense: Simple Past Tense, Past Progressive Tense, Past Perfect Tense. Given below are some verbs from the story. Put the verbs in their correct column in the table given above: was looking, looked, gave, had ordered, was missing, hated, had followed, noticed, was munching, went, had remained, served, told, asked, felt, touched, had tied, joined, arrived, threw.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Select the correct form of the verb to complete the passage. Check your answers with the teacher. The farmer came back and ____________ (noticed/was noticing/had noticed) that Buntee was sad. He ____________ (tied/was tying/had tied) Buntee with a rope the day before. He ____________ (asked/was asking/had asked) Buntee why he was sad. The farmer ____________ (felt/was feeling/had felt) that Buntee ____________ (missed/was missing/had missed) Gajaraj and set him free.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: '…neither the farmer nor the mahout noticed that the two friends were in tears.' The words 'neither', 'nor' are used to state that the farmer and the mahout did not notice the same thing. 'Neither, nor' connect two negative choices. When you need to choose between two things that you want, you may use 'either, or'. Discuss the given sentences with your teacher to practise: Neither Shikha nor Anuj likes to tell stories. / Either Bhavesh or Abha made this painting.

question_bank.stem: Discuss and practise the use of neither/nor and either/or using the given example sentences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The story, 'The Elephant and the Dog' is found in the Jataka tales. These stories are ancient Buddhist stories of life values and wisdom. These stories have been told from one generation to the next for over two thousand years. Find and read another Jataka story.

Elephants have historically held significant roles in Indian culture, spanning history, warfare, religion, festivals and more. However, contemporary ecological challenges have strained the relationship between humans and elephants. Have a discussion in the class.

Given below is a news report from an Elephant Sanctuary in the United States about a real-life friendship between an elephant and a dog. The elephant's name is Tarra and the dog's name is Bella. Bella came inside a forest for elephants and became Tarra's friend. Tarra spent most of her time with Bella. One day, Bella had an injury so she could not walk. She was kept inside a building for three weeks. Tarra stood outside for three weeks. Finally, they met and were very happy.

question_bank.stem: Read and discuss the real-life news report about Tarra the elephant and Bella the dog.

main_sections.title: A Friend's Prayer
main_sections.poem_text:
May my friendships always be
The most important thing to me.

With special friends I feel I'm blessed,
So let me give my very best.

I want to do much more than share
The hopes and plans of friends who care;
I'll try all that a friend can do
To make their wishes come true.

Let me use my heart to see,
To realise what friends can be,

And make no judgements from afar,
But love my friends the way they are.

— Jill Wolf
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: This short lyric by Jill Wolf is written in the form of a heartfelt prayer addressed to no specific deity but to the idea of friendship itself. The dominant theme is selfless devotion: the speaker wishes not merely to share in friendship but to actively work to make her friends' wishes come true. The tone is warm, earnest, and quietly aspirational. Central imagery includes the heart as an organ of understanding rather than emotion — 'Let me use my heart to see' — suggesting empathy as the true basis of friendship. The poem's most striking idea is unconditional acceptance: 'love my friends the way they are' and 'make no judgements from afar.' The rhyme scheme (AABB) gives the poem the cadence of a simple prayer or vow, reinforcing its devotional quality.

As a good friend, she prays/imagines that she is able to make her friends' wishes come true. She wants her heart/mind to understand what a true friend is. She prays that she loves her friends as they are/when they are happy. She tells us that true/correct friends accept their friends for all their qualities.

listening.transcript_text: Hello! Let me tell you what outdoor activities Ajay and Dhiraj enjoy doing. Some days they like playing at the swings in the park. Ajay enjoys slides the most. Dhiraj likes nature, so both take walks in the nearby park, listen to birds and look at the trees. On Saturdays, both go cycling around the neighbourhood. What both truly enjoy is eating ice-cream together. Sometimes they also purchase a comic book, sit on a park bench and read together. They are truly best friends.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In pairs, act the role of friends speaking to each other. Speak about the following situations: 1. A walk in the park: Greet each other. Ask if your friend wishes to go with you to the park. If yes, plan some activities. If no, ask what your friend would like to do. Listen and share what you would also like to do together. 2. Sharing tiffins at school: Greet each other. Tell your friends what food items you have brought in your tiffin. Ask them what they have brought. Tell them you wish to share tiffins! Next, share with your friends, how it makes you feel. Also: Tell your friend that you like spending time with each other. Appreciate your friends' food and ask what other food items they like. Thank your friends for sharing.
Create a new situation and have another conversation.

Describe how your friendship with your friend started. First list the answers for — When? Where? How? Then, use your sentences to write a short paragraph of about 80 words. Give a title to your paragraph.

What do you think is the meaning of these quotations? Share it with your teacher. 2. Think of an experience in your life with your friend that suits this quotation. Share it with your friends.

main_sections.title: The Chair
main_sections.prose_summary: Mario is a boastful boy who constantly brags about the enormous number of friends he has at school. His wise grandfather challenges him with a wager — a fruit chaat — betting that most of his supposed friends are merely companions or partners, not true friends. To help Mario discover the truth, grandfather produces an invisible chair from the attic, explaining that anyone who can sit on this tricky object will reveal who his real friends are through the magic it performs. Mario bravely takes the invisible chair to school at break time and attempts to sit on it in front of a circle of classmates. He falls repeatedly, to general laughter. On one final attempt, however, he finds himself seated and supported — looking around, he sees that three friends, Guneet, Asma, and Deepa, have silently moved behind him and are holding him up. The rest of the class did nothing but mock his falls. Mario leaves school with his three true friends and explains the grandfather's clever lesson: true friends are those who care for us and would never take joy in our bad luck. That evening, all four children visit grandfather, who has won the bet. They eat fruit chaat, listen to stories, and from that day on use the invisible chair test whenever they want to identify friends for life. The story is a light-hearted but pointed fable about the difference between genuine friendship and mere acquaintance, using the conceit of an invisible chair as a metaphor for the invisible tests of loyalty every friendship must pass.

listening.transcript_text: When I first joined this new school, things were very difficult. There was Monika. She was a supportive friend who guided me, introduced me to others and even shared her notes. That made me comfortable in the school. Since then, Monika and I have been together through difficult times and happy times. She is the kind of friend who is always there for you. I'm really thankful to her, and how her help and support turned into a really good friendship.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the following task by talking to different people of different age groups as mentioned in the table. Ask: 'What does friendship mean to you?' to people in age groups 50+, 31 to 49, 20 to 30, 12 to 19. After gathering responses: Discuss similarities and differences across age groups. Reflect on how viewpoints on friendship may change with age and life experiences. Present a summary of the group's responses, emphasising key themes or unique insights.

question_bank.stem: Interview people from four age groups (50+, 31-49, 20-30, 12-19) on what friendship means to them. Record responses in the table.
